refined: Cleanup after the iOS sign-in investigation: removed the temporary diagnostics and restored the pre-auth cross-site protection to its strict setting. No user-facing change.
archivist_note: The iOS sign-in saga resolved at the edge: privacy iOS browsers were reaching the site over HTTP, and a Secure session cookie can't persist over HTTP — Cloudflare "Always Use HTTPS" closed it. The detour through CSRF and cookies hardened the pre-auth path on the way, and the proxy-awareness fix that finally exposed the real cause is keeping it honest.

refined: Internal: extended the iOS sign-in diagnostic (a login-success marker + a session-cookie-clone probe) to pinpoint the remaining cause. No user-facing change.
Internal: a temporary diagnostic probe to pinpoint why some iOS browsers don't keep the sign-in session. No user-facing change.

Internal: the app now correctly detects HTTPS behind the reverse proxy (a foundation fix for the iOS sign-in investigation), and the session cookie setting reverted to its standard value. No user-facing change.

resolved: Sign-in on Firefox for iOS (and similar privacy browsers) succeeded but immediately bounced back to the splash because the session cookie wasn't being kept. The session cookie now uses a setting (SameSite=None) those browsers retain, so signing in sticks.

refined: Internal diagnostics to investigate a sign-in persistence issue on privacy-focused browsers (login succeeds but the session doesn't stick). No user-facing change.

resolved: Hotfix: v4.1.5's new sign-in protection incorrectly blocked logins behind the reverse proxy ("Cross-site request blocked"). The origin check is now advisory so sign-in works reliably again, with the signed-token protection still enforced.

Sign-in and registration could fail on privacy-focused iOS browsers (Firefox for iOS, Opera Touch): the four public auth forms (login, register, forgot-password, reset-password) no longer depend on a session cookie those browsers drop when submitting, so they now work everywhere.

refined: Scryfall ingest fails every cycle: full_art boolean written to integer column (Postgres DatatypeMismatch)
Extend no_session diagnostics with Origin/Referer presence and same-origin match to validate the #63 cross-site gate
Add diagnostic logging at the CSRF no_session branch to capture client/network signal on "session expired" failures
Each deck on the Decks page now shows its Total Value — the summed market price of the cards in it.

Behind-the-scenes reliability: database schema changes now apply through a dedicated migration step that runs before each release rolls out, instead of the app building tables on startup. This closes a class of silent migration drift and makes deploys safer. No visible change to the app itself.

added: Per-deck goals now track completion: when you finish a game, mark which of that deck's goals you achieved, and each deck's page shows how often you've hit each one (e.g. 3 of 8 games, 38%). Builds on the deck goals added in the previous release.

refined: You can now set **goals** for each deck — a custom list of what you're trying to do with it, kept separate from your win/loss record. Manage them from the deck edit screen; a deck's active goals show on its page.

You can now correct a collection card's finish (Normal, Foil, or Etched) in place from the collection page, keeping its location, tags, deck links, and history intact instead of deleting and re-adding it.

Card prices now come from a daily MTGJSON ingest with a tcgplayer, cardkingdom, cardsphere fallback (USD providers only) and per-printing manual overrides, replacing Scryfall pricing for more accurate valuations.

Cartarch can now generate a legal Commander Brew from your owned collection: choose a commander, preview the recommended decklist with reasons and warnings, then save it as a Brew Mode deck without moving any physical cards.

---

**Labels:** `feature` (add `recommendations` and `deckbuilding` only if those labels already exist or you want to start using them consistently)

Collection and deck exports can now emit JSON with full card details (colors,
types, mana value, legality, rules text), so external tools and assistants can
read your cards directly without looking each one up. The collection CSV also
gains color, type, and mana-value columns.

The admin panel now shows when each user was last active, in addition to when
they last signed in.
The internal metrics endpoint is now access-restricted and no longer publicly visible.
Fixed the import destination dropdown showing an incorrect label for binder locations.
Fixed duplicate entry numbers appearing in the Chronicle.
Fixed the Goldfish card preview being dimmed when a modal is open.
Fixed the alignment of the brew/proxy checkbox on the Decks page.

Decks in a variant group now show the full shared decklist. Shared cards appear in both decks with a badge indicating they're shared from a sibling deck. Your collection count stays accurate — shared cards are only counted once.

resolved: Fixed an intermittent "session expired" error when signing in on Firefox after using the back button or returning to a left-open login page.
Card imports now enforce size limits (2 MB / 5,000 lines) to prevent oversized uploads.

refined: Added a version indicator to the mobile UI so the running release is identifiable on-device.
Fixed grammar and spelling issues in app text for clarity and consistency.
Added rate limiting to the login page. Repeated failed login attempts are now temporarily throttled to protect accounts.
Fixed recent games screen to only show games the user was part of.

resolved: Game statistics no longer count every game as a win — only the games you actually won are tallied as wins, and other outcomes are categorized correctly.

refined: Cards on the Cull page now sort by collector number in the correct numeric order (1, 2, 10 instead of 1, 10, 2).

resolved: On phones, the Edit Deck pop-up is back to its proper card look — bordered, rounded, and neatly padded — while keeping the brightness improvements. This closes the Edit Deck pop-up readability issue across phone and desktop.
archivist_note: The Edit Deck pop-up took a few passes to get fully right: brightness first, then a fix so it stopped dimming itself behind its own shaded background, and finally restoring the card framing that fix had briefly stripped away. It now reads cleanly everywhere.

resolved: On phones, the Edit Deck pop-up no longer appears washed out by its own shaded background — it now opens as a clean, centered card above the dimmed page.

refined: The Edit Deck pop-up on desktop now uses the same brightened, easy-to-read panel the phone version received — the earlier contrast fix had only reached mobile.
On phones, the form labels in pop-up edit windows (like Edit Deck) are now bright and easy to read against the lightened panel introduced in the last update.
Fixed the Edit Deck modal rendering too dark and hard to read.

resolved: Deck list export now records whether each card is foil or etched, so exporting a deck and importing it back keeps the right finish instead of resetting every card to non-foil.
Goldfish: when you move a card out of the "look at the top of your library" list, it now leaves that list right away instead of lingering until you close and reopen it.
Collection: searching, filtering, or adding a card no longer snaps you back to the top of the page — your scroll position stays where you left it.
archivist_note: Two small frictions smoothed — one at the goldfishing table, one on the collection shelf. We're also chasing a rarer one: a few Firefox players have been quietly logged out mid-session. We haven't pinned it down yet, but the investigation is underway, and the early evidence points at the in-progress move between homes rather than anything in the app itself.

refined: CSV exports are hardened against spreadsheet formula injection: a cell that starts with =, +, -, or @ in your own text (a location name or tag) is quoted so the spreadsheet opens it as plain text, not a live formula.
resolved: The dashboard Spotlight no longer features a proxy at full market price — it features your most recent real card instead.
Games now appear on the Decks page for everyone who played, not just the person who logged the game.
archivist_note: The first patch after the big move — a few sharp edges smoothed now that the collection sits on its new foundation. Nothing flashy: a proxy that shouldn't have been bragging about its price, a games tally that only counted the scorekeeper, and a quiet hardening of the export so a clever card name can't run as a spreadsheet formula.

refined: Cartarch's collection now lives in PostgreSQL instead of a single SQLite file — the same app and the same data, on a sturdier foundation built for what comes next.
archivist_note: Cartarch IV. No new buttons, nothing to relearn — by design. This release is the move itself: the database changed underneath, the application stayed exactly as you left it, and the whole thing now runs on a new home cluster. The kind of release you only notice by the quiet that follows it.

refined: Locked down the web-framework dependencies so a routine rebuild can no longer pull in a newer-but-incompatible version and quietly break the site.
resolved: Re-landed the data-integrity fixes from earlier in the day, for real this time — the small reference-cleanup gaps that had been slowly accruing stop here.
archivist_note: The fix for the morning's stumble, and the last release before the big move. The lesson banked: pin the whole stack, because the thing that breaks you is rarely the dependency you changed — it's the one underneath it that moved on its own. With this, the foundation was finally still enough to migrate.

resolved: More of the behind-the-scenes cleanup that keeps a card from leaving a ghost behind when it's moved or an import is undone — continuing the reference-tidying from the previous releases so the collection stays consistent on the way to its new home.
archivist_note: Pure groundwork for the database move, no new buttons. Honest footnote: the build that carried it tripped over an unrelated dependency that had quietly drifted to a newer, incompatible version, so it was pulled back within the hour and re-shipped — properly pinned — as v3.39.9 the same day. A stumble caught fast.

added: You can now delete cards in bulk from the Collection page. Open "Bulk actions", narrow the filter to the cards you want gone (or leave it empty for the whole collection), and "Delete matching" shows a preview first — how many rows and cards, and how many are in decks, in showcases, and in showcases you've shared — before you confirm. It acts on everything matching the filter across all pages, never just the page you're looking at. Deleting your entire collection additionally asks you to type DELETE.
archivist_note: The last piece of the collection-delete work: a real "wipe and redo" for the day an import goes sideways. Built on the reference-cleanup plumbing from the previous release, so a deleted card leaves a shared binder cleanly instead of as a dangling ghost — and the preview tells you the blast radius before you commit, not after.

refined: The Collection bulk-actions panel now reads "N rows (M cards)" — it had been labeling a count of inventory rows as "cards," which never matched the dashboard's card total.
resolved: Fixed a bug where moving a card between locations, or undoing an import, could silently drop that card out of a Showcase you'd shared or a trade you'd proposed — the reference was left dangling and the card just vanished from the other person's view with no warning. Those paths now clean up the references the same way an ordinary delete already did. A one-time maintenance sweep also cleared the small number of already-dangling references the old behavior had left behind (dev came back clean).
archivist_note: Plumbing more than features. Those dangling references looked harmless under the current database but would have hard-failed the move to the new one — so this is a real bug fix and a brick laid for the v4 cutover at once. The reference cleanup now lives in a single place that every delete path shares.

added: You can now add a card to your Wishlist by searching for it. Type any card name — owned or not — in the new "Add a card" box on the Wishlist page, pick it from the dropdown, and it's added as an any-printing Wishlist row. Adding the same card twice just tells you it's already there.
resolved: Fixed a gap that left a brand-new account with no way to add its first Wishlist card. The empty-Wishlist hint pointed only at browsing Sets — but the Sets page is empty until you own cards, so a fresh account had no path in at all. The search box is now the front door; Sets browsing stays as a secondary route.
archivist_note: The kind of gap you only see from an empty account. Every test we'd run used a collection full of cards, which quietly hid the fact that a first-day user had nowhere to start. Now the Wishlist works the moment you arrive — before you've imported a thing.

refined: Proxies in a shared binder or a trade now carry $0.00 and a clear amber PROXY flag instead of the real card's market price. They're still allowed — list a proxy to give away or trade it as a proxy — but a shared list's total and a trade's value no longer count it. Bulk-adding a whole collection or location to a showcase (and the Collection "add matching" action) now skips proxies unless you tick "Include proxies (listed at $0)".
resolved: Trades never actually flagged proxies. A proxy offered or requested in a trade showed no PROXY badge and was priced at full market value — so a $34 "proxy" read just like the real card on a proposal. Trades now flag proxies, value them at $0, and show a one-line notice when a trade includes any.
archivist_note: A trust fix more than a feature. At a real table, a proxy listed at the genuine card's price is a way to be misread — honestly or not. Showcases and trades now say the quiet part out loud: a proxy is a proxy, and it's worth $0 to the person across the table.

added: Two new shortcuts on the dashboard — Showcases and Trades — so the trading features are one click from the home page.
A "Share with a playgroup" button right on each Showcase, so you can share it without first hunting for the Shares page.
refined: The Showcases page now says what it's for — a trade binder, a show-off shelf, a sale pile — instead of leaving you to guess. The card-detail "Watch" controls and the Storage "Binder" type were the two words new players tripped over: Storage now reads "Binder (physical)" so it isn't mistaken for a trade binder. The read-only notice on a shared Showcase moved up under the title, where you'll actually see it before scrolling.
resolved: New players kept hitting a dead end trying to trade. The rule that makes trading work — a playmate has to share a Showcase to a playgroup you're both in — was only spelled out on the propose-a-trade screen. It now appears on the Playgroup and Trades pages too, with a nudge to share one of your own to get things moving.
archivist_note: Everything in this release already worked; it just wasn't findable. A week alongside a new playgroup made the gaps plain — people went looking for a "binder," landed in Storage, and never found Showcases. So this is a signposting pass: the same machinery, with clearer doors.

refined: The Watchlist is now the Wishlist. Same feature — a private list of cards you want to acquire, with optional price targets — renamed to say what it's for. The page now leads with hunting cards for upgrades, brews, or someday, with price-target alerts as the secondary note. On a card's page the button now reads "Add to Wishlist" instead of "Watch this printing."
resolved: Fixed the empty Wishlist's getting-started hint. It used to tell you to find a card via your Collection — circular if your collection is empty. It now points you to browse Sets, which reaches any card's page whether you own it or not, where you can add it to your Wishlist.
archivist_note: A rename that's really a clarification. "Watchlist" read like a price ticker, so players hunting cards for their next deck never thought to open it. "Wishlist" is the word they were already looking for — so now the feature is found by the people it was built for.

added: Pasting a card list now resolves cards by name alone — a line like "1 Mizzix of the Izmagnus" with no set or collector number just works, picking the default printing. Add a set in parentheses, e.g. "1 Mizzix of the Izmagnus (C15)", to pin a specific one. It resolves a whole pasted list of names in one batched lookup, so even long lists come back fast.
refined: When a pasted name can't be found, the message now points at the likely cause — a misspelling, or a card that needs a set + collector to disambiguate — instead of flatly refusing names. A line that already names a specific set and collector still resolves to exactly that printing and is never quietly swapped for a different one.
resolved: Removed the old "bare card names can't be batch-resolved" wall on the paste importer. Name-only lines used to be rejected with a note to add a set + collector or use the single-card "Import by name" tool; now they resolve directly in the batch import.
archivist_note: The single-card importer could always find a card by name; the batch importer couldn't, and said so. It turned out the batch lookup could take names all along — we'd just never asked it to. So now a pasted list of plain names lands the same way one careful single card does.

refined: Pasting a deck list into a brew now recognizes cards you own in any printing — if you own a card, it gets pulled into the brew instead of being marked "to buy," even when the list names a different printing than the one in your drawers. Cards you own only inside another deck now show as "owned — in another deck" rather than something to purchase.

The import preview no longer silently skips lines it can't read. Set codes that start with a number (40K, 2X2) and collector numbers with dashes or stars (2026-1, ★) now parse, and any line it still can't match is listed for you to acknowledge before the import runs.
resolved: Deleting a deck no longer destroys the cards in it. Your real cards return to the collection — drawer-sorted users get them re-filed to their drawers automatically — and only the proxies are discarded. Building a brew and then tearing it down now leaves your collection exactly as it was.
archivist_note: A brew should know what you already own — in any printing, in any drawer — and giving a deck back should give the cards back too. This release closes both gaps: the buy-list stops crying wolf over cards you own, and disbanding a deck stopped quietly eating them.

added: Drawer-vs-Bulk routing. When you import a cheap, non-staple card you already keep a copy of in your drawers, the extra copies now go to a Bulk box automatically instead of taking up prime drawer space — one findable copy always stays in the drawers. Basics, anything you run in a deck, and anything worth more than $1 are always kept. The import preview tells you up front how many copies are headed to the drawers versus Bulk.

A matching "cull cheap drawer dupes to Bulk" action on the Collection page does the same housekeeping for cards already sitting in your drawers — surplus copies of cheap non-staples move to Bulk, one of each stays. It shows a confirmation screen with exactly what will move before anything happens, and you can sweep every drawer at once or just the one you're looking at.
archivist_note: The drawers are for finding things; bulk is for everything else. This release teaches the collection that distinction — the same judgment applied whether a card is arriving or already in a slot — without touching the hand-tuned sorter underneath it.

refined: The brew buy-list now reads a brew's own cards directly: a real copy in the deck counts as owned, a proxy counts as "to buy." Whether your single physical copy sits in the brew or in a drawer no longer matters.
resolved: Fixed brew buy-lists wrongly listing cards you own as "to buy." Any card you owned just one copy of got flagged to buy the moment you put it in the brew — because building the deck moved your only copy into it. Importing a deck-list into a brew now also marks the cards you don't own as proxies, so owned and to-buy come out right (the Mizzix brew now reads 93 owned / 7 to buy instead of 31 / 47).

added: Brew Mode for decks. Flip on the new "Brew" toggle when you create or edit a deck, and it becomes a place to build with cards you don't own yet — for testing ideas before you buy. When you add a card you don't own to a brew, Cartarch automatically marks it as a proxy so it never inflates your real collection totals, and the deck page shows a buy-list: what you still need, what you partly have, and what you already own a real copy of elsewhere (your proxies and the brew's own cards don't count as owned). A "Brew" badge marks these decks throughout.
refined: The owned-vs-missing comparison that powers the existing Decklist Check is now the single shared engine behind the brew buy-list too, so the two can never disagree about what you own.
archivist_note: Alex asked in passing whether Cartarch could hold a deck of cards you don't own — for brewing. It could, sort of, but only by hand. This makes it a first-class mode: a brew keeps unowned cards as proxies so your collection stays honest, and tells you exactly what it would cost to make the deck real. It is also the first deck-table change in a long, deliberately quiet run of releases — a small, careful step taken with the coming database move already in mind.

refined: Polished the Pending Placement page: the set code now shows the card's collector number beside it (e.g. "SOA #58"), and that label is a clickable link straight to the card's Scryfall page. The Confirm and Remove buttons no longer sit flush against the card's outline — each pending card now has comfortable padding so the controls breathe inside their border.

A behind-the-scenes cleanup pass with no visible changes to how Cartarch looks or works. We removed duplicated and dead code, tidied how the app starts up and shuts down, and lifted the entire card-import section out of the app's most crowded file into its own home — all groundwork to keep things healthy and quick to change as we prepare for the upcoming database upgrade. The full test suite passed unchanged at every step, confirming nothing about your collection, decks, or imports behaves differently.
archivist_note: Not every release is something you can see. This one was a deliberate tidying-up before the larger move to a new database: we deleted an old scoring routine that had quietly drifted out of step with its replacement, folded several near-identical copies of the same logic back into single shared pieces, and moved the whole card-import flow out of the app's busiest file — which shrank from roughly 2,800 lines to about 1,200. We also added a small health check the servers can ping to know the app is ready. None of this changes your day to day; it just makes the next, larger step safer to take.

added: Your collection CSV export now includes two new columns: Price (the current value of each card, for its finish) and Scryfall ID. The price makes the export a ready-made worksheet — sort by value to build a “surplus bulk” pull list — and the Scryfall ID is a stable key for joining the file against other tools.
refined: Exporting your collection now respects whatever you’ve filtered the page down to. Narrow the view to a single drawer, one color, or everything under a dollar, and the downloaded file contains exactly those cards. Sorting and searching already travelled with the page; now the export does too.
resolved: The CSV export used to ignore your active filters and always download your entire collection, no matter how carefully you’d narrowed the page. It now exports the filtered view you actually see.
archivist_note: The page and its export button had quietly grown apart — the page understood your filters, the export didn’t — so “export” still meant “export everything” even while the screen showed a careful slice. We gave the two a single, shared idea of what you’re looking at, so they can never disagree again, and slipped a price beside each card so a download can leave as a working list rather than a flat inventory.

added: A Sort control is now on every page that lists cards — your Collection, each Deck, every storage Location, and your Showcases and shared views. Pick a field (Name, Mana Value, Color, Set, Rarity, Price, or Quantity Available) and a direction (ascending or descending), and the list reorders without disturbing your active search or filters. Sorting and searching now work together: changing the sort keeps your search, and searching keeps your sort. The choice rides along in the page address, so it survives a refresh — but it isn't saved as a preference, so every page starts in its familiar default order until you change it.
refined: Sorting that used to be a guess is now exact: Rarity sorts common → uncommon → rare → mythic (not alphabetically), Color follows the usual W-U-B-R-G order with multicolour then colourless after, and cards with no price drop to the end instead of masquerading as the cheapest.
resolved: Showcases and shared views had no real order — newly added cards led, but cards added together (for example when you shared a whole shelf at once) fell in an unpredictable order that could shuffle between visits. They now hold a steady, predictable order, and you can sort them however you like.
archivist_note: Five different pages had each grown their own idea of sorting — one had every option, two had a few, two had none at all — and the one we trusted most turned out to be ordering a showcase by nothing in particular. So we did the unglamorous thing: wrote the rules down once, in a single place, and taught every page to read from it. The reward is consistency you shouldn't have to think about — Rarity means the same thing on your Collection as it does on a deck, and equal cards stop quietly trading places when the page redraws. The hardest calls were the ones with no tidy answer: a card's colours aren't a single value, 'rarity' isn't alphabetical, and a missing price isn't the same as a free card. We picked an order for each, on purpose, and now they hold everywhere.

resolved: The deck card auto-tagger now recognises two kinds of card it had been quietly missing. Creatures and enchantments that deal damage equal to a creature's power when something enters the battlefield — like Warstorm Surge and Terror of the Peaks — are now tagged as Threats. And repeatable 'tap to draw a card' engines that switch on once a condition is met — like Idol of Oblivion — are now tagged as card-draw Engines. Tags on cards you already own update the next time the tagger runs over them.
archivist_note: Both gaps were quiet ones: the rules to catch these cards existed, but each was written just slightly askew of how the cards are actually worded, so they matched nothing. A new test pass — checking the tagger against real card text rather than card names — caught both, and the fixes were small once the misalignment was visible. The lesson keeps: a rule is only as good as the words it actually reads.

added: Bulk actions on the Collection page. Filter your collection however you like — a search, a colour, a price range, a guild name like `id:izzet` — then add every matching card to a showcase, or move them all to a storage location, in a single step. It acts on the whole matching set across every page, not just the cards on screen. Cards still waiting to be placed (pending) are left out, and the number skipped is shown, so nothing ever moves silently. A confirmation step guards the move.
archivist_note: The collection page already knew how to narrow thousands of cards to the handful you meant. What it couldn't do was act on that selection as a whole — you saw your izzet spellslingers, then filed them one by one. Now the filter and the action meet: describe a set, and send all of it to a showcase or a shelf at once. The page acts on every match, not merely the fifty it happens to be showing, and it tells you plainly what it left behind.

resolved: Searching your collection by a colour-pair or guild name now works. A search like `id:izzet` or `c:azorius` used to either return everything or quietly return the wrong cards, because the search box only understood the single colour letters (W, U, B, R, G) and not the colour names players actually use. It now recognises all ten guild names, the five shards and five wedges, the mono-colour names, and "colorless" — so `id:izzet` correctly finds your blue-red cards. Typing the raw letters (`id:ur`) still works exactly as before.
archivist_note: A search box ought to speak the language of the people using it. Magic players reach for guild names — izzet, azorius, golgari — far more readily than for letters, yet the filter quietly understood only the letters and made a silent guess at the rest, sometimes returning everything, sometimes nothing of use. Now the names resolve to their colours before the search runs, and a request for an izzet card returns an izzet card. The finer grammar of comparison — "at most these colours" versus "exactly these" — remains a task for another day.

refined: The automatic drawer sorter now files oversized cards — Planechase planes and phenomena, and Archenemy schemes and vanguards — at the very back of Drawer 6. These cards are physically larger than a normal card and don't sit neatly in the regular slots, so grouping them together at the back keeps the drawer tidy. Planeswalkers (despite the similar name) are unaffected. Cards already filed elsewhere move to their new home the next time you auto-sort or apply the sorter.
archivist_note: Some cards simply refuse to fit. The big Planechase and Archenemy cards have always sat awkwardly among their letter-sorted neighbors, so the sorter now gathers them where size, not set code, decides the address: the back of the last drawer. A small courtesy to anyone who has ever tried to wedge a plane between two ordinary cards.

refined: Two more invisible portability fixes ahead of the database upgrade. (1) A pair of internal catalogue lookups that match card types by text now use a case-insensitive match explicitly, so they keep working on the new database engine, which (unlike the current one) treats upper- and lower-case as different. (2) A background price-refresh queue now states its sort order explicitly, so cards are refreshed in the same priority order on both the current and new engines. Neither changes anything you can see today.
archivist_note: The current database engine is forgiving in ways the next one is not — it ignores letter case in text matches and quietly orders empty values one way where the successor orders them the other. Each such difference is a trapdoor that opens only after the migration. This release nails two of them shut while they're still cheap to reach, and checks the catalogue to confirm one of them couldn't have sprung at all.

refined: More groundwork for the coming database upgrade. The price search filter (e.g. 'price:>=5') reads card prices that are stored as text and converts them to numbers on the fly. The current database treats a blank price as zero; the new one would instead reject the whole search with an error. We checked every price in the collection first — all 12,753 cards are clean, no blanks or bad values — and then added a guard that turns any stray blank into 'no price' so the search keeps working after the move. No visible change today.
archivist_note: Two kinds of diligence in one release: we looked before we leapt — auditing every stored price and finding them all sound — and then fitted the safety rail anyway, for the one blank value that doesn't exist yet but someday might. The audit said 'you don't strictly need this'; we built it regardless, because the cost of the rail is a line of code and the cost of its absence is a broken search the day after the migration.

refined: Groundwork for the coming database upgrade. One command that builds the dashboard's per-deck colour summaries was written in a dialect only the current database engine understands; we swapped it for a portable equivalent that works on both the current engine and the one we're migrating to. Nothing changes on screen — the dashboard renders exactly as before — but one more thing that would have broken during the move is now safe ahead of time.
archivist_note: A database migration is made of many small, invisible repairs like this one — each a spot where the old code spoke a private dialect. Found and swapped before the move it costs nothing; found during the move it would be an outage. This is the first of the catalogued few being cleared in advance, and the dashboard never noticed.

resolved: Fixed the planeswalker/battle starting-counter feature from the last release, which wasn't actually populating: the goldfish playtester now reliably stamps a planeswalker's loyalty (and a battle's defense) when it enters, for the cards you already own. The values are read straight from the source so the feature works on deploy regardless of when the card catalogue last refreshed.
archivist_note: A feature that ships but does nothing is just a rumor of a feature. The previous release taught the playtester to set a planeswalker's loyalty automatically — but it was reading those numbers from a local catalogue that hadn't yet been refreshed with them, so it quietly set nothing. This release has it read the numbers from the source directly, so it no longer depends on that refresh having happened first. Small, surgical, and the right place to end a long thread of related work: the goldfish wishlist is complete, and our attention now turns to the larger move — the sturdier database that several devices can share at once.

added: In the goldfish playtester, planeswalkers and battles now enter the battlefield with their starting counters already set — a planeswalker arrives at its printed loyalty, a battle at its printed defense — so you don't have to dial them in by hand. Adjust them like any other counter; they hold steady through a new turn and reset when you start a new game. (A permanent that leaves and comes back returns at its printed value, just like the real rules.) Cards we don't have a printed value for simply get no auto-counter — you can still add one yourself.
archivist_note: This is the last stone in a small arch we've been building for the goldfish table. Over the past few releases the playtester learned to track an opponent's life, to let you re-sort and re-stat a permanent by hand, and — quietly, underneath — to remember two facts about cards it had been ignoring. Today those facts come to the surface: cast a planeswalker and it stands up at its real loyalty; bring in a battle and its defense is already counting down. The work that makes this possible is deliberately humble — the program reads what it already knows and stamps the number once, then gets out of your way. We even taught the catalogue to fill in those numbers for the cards you already own, so the feature works on your whole collection and not just on cards added from here on. With this, the goldfish wishlist that started all of it is complete.

Groundwork, plainly: nothing in this release changes a screen. We taught the card catalogue to remember two more facts it had been ignoring — a planeswalker's starting loyalty and a battle's defense — and made sure they flow quietly into the goldfish playtester's data without any extra lookups to the outside world. They sit there unused for now, on purpose. The next step will let the playtester start a planeswalker at its printed loyalty on its own, instead of asking you to dial it in by hand; this release is the plumbing that makes that possible. Existing cards fill in the new facts over the next day or so as the catalogue refreshes, and the playtester behaves exactly as before until then.

added: The goldfish playtester now lets you hand-edit any permanent on the battlefield. A new "Move to region…" option on a card's menu moves it between the board's areas — Creatures, Lands, Artifacts & Enchantments, Planeswalkers & Battles, or Other — so you can, say, treat an animated artifact as a creature for the rest of the game. Your choice sticks (and survives the card leaving and re-entering play) until you start a new game.

Power and toughness changes are right where you'd expect, too: the existing "Counters…" panel already carries +1/+1 and -1/-1, and they stack into the running total shown on the card (two +1/+1 counters read as +2/+2). Nothing new to learn — we just confirmed it works cleanly on creatures and left it alone.
archivist_note: A goldfishing table is a thinking space, not a rules court, and this release leans into that. The playtester has always sorted your board into tidy areas by reading each card's type — but cards lie, or rather, they change: an artifact stands up and swings, a creature is, for the moment, just a wall. So we handed the sorting back to you. A card's menu now offers to move it to whichever area you say, and it remembers, because you had a reason. We resisted the urge to make the program clever about it — no detecting, no second-guessing — since the whole pleasure of goldfishing is being the one who decides. And the well-worn counters that track a creature's swelling power needed nothing but a nod: they were already doing the job.

added: The goldfish playtester now has a dedicated opponent-life tracker. It counts down from a starting total you can flip between 20 and 40 (40 by default, since this corner of the app leans Commander), and it sits in the top bar next to your own life — tinted differently so the two are never mixed up. Crucially, it stays put when you start a new turn (it only resets when you start a new game), so you can finally answer "how many turns to lethal?" without borrowing the mana counter, which clears every turn.
archivist_note: Every tool gets pressed into service it wasn't built for. Goldfishing a deck — playing it solo to see how fast it can kill — the mana counter kept getting drafted as a damage tally, and kept betraying its conscript: mana empties at the top of each turn, as it should, taking the running total with it. So rather than teach the mana pool to hoard, we gave the table the counter it was actually reaching for — an opponent's life total that ticks down, holds its ground turn to turn, and only forgets when a new game begins. It starts at forty, the Commander default, but flips to twenty for the duels; it allows going below zero and back, because a misclick shouldn't cost you the count. A small dial on the dashboard, but the right one.

resolved: Fixed the goldfish playtester's "New turn" button: it now advances and repaints the turn counter the moment you click it. Previously, if you'd turned off automatic draws, the counter sat still until your next "Draw" and then jumped ahead all at once.
archivist_note: A small thing the eye notices immediately: you press "New turn" and nothing moves. The turn was advancing all along — the screen simply wasn't being told to redraw unless a card was also drawn, a side effect we'd quietly leaned on and then, months ago, removed without noticing. So the counter saved up its changes and spent them all at once on the next draw. The repair is a single line restoring the redraw to where it belongs. No saved game was touched, no in-progress tracker disturbed — just the button doing, visibly, what it always claimed to.

refined: More groundwork for the larger database upgrade ahead: two internal queries behind deck power-level analysis were rewritten to behave identically on both today's database and the sturdier one we're moving to — with no visible change to the app.
archivist_note: A short run of releases — 3.34.1 through 3.34.5 — spent entirely on the unglamorous work that makes a safe move possible. None of it moves a button or changes a screen. We taught the code to speak to both the current database and its successor without having to choose between them; we swept the whole collection for the kind of broken internal reference the new database would reject, and found almost none — five, all harmless, among tens of thousands of records; and we retired an old code-formatting tool that had quietly been tripping our own automated checks. Foundations, not façades — laid now, deliberately, so that the move itself can be boring.

refined: Repaired our internal automated-checks pipeline, which had been failing after an old code-formatting tool was retired. Behind-the-scenes tooling only.

Database-upgrade groundwork: the app now adapts automatically to whichever database it is running on, and we added an internal tool that scans the entire collection for broken links the new database wouldn't accept — it found only five, all harmless.
Tightened our pre-release quality checks and added an internal audit command to help plan the database upgrade. Behind-the-scenes tooling only.

Reorganized the application's internal code: the routes that handle games, decks, your collection, and card/token/set pages moved out of one oversized file into four clean, separate modules. Nothing about how the app looks or behaves changed — this is groundwork for the larger database upgrade ahead that will let several devices share a game live.
archivist_note: Not every chapter is written for the reader; some are written so the next ones can be. This release moves no button and changes no screen — it tidies the workshop. The single file that had grown to carry most of the application's traffic was divided into four orderly rooms, each holding one concern, so that the far larger work ahead — moving the collection onto a sturdier database that many devices can use at once — can begin on solid ground instead of in a crowded hallway. We shipped it on its own and let it settle, precisely so that if anything stirs we will know it was the moving and not the foundation.

resolved: Opening a finished game now shows a proper recap — final standings, who won, turn count, how long it ran, and the complete notes — instead of a frozen, do-nothing version of the life tracker. New games also record how long they took to play.
archivist_note: A reader of recorded games pointed out, rightly, that a finished match had nowhere to actually read its ending — clicking through dropped you into the life tracker with its buttons greyed out, a tool with nothing left to do. So finished games now open as a record rather than a frozen instrument: the final standings in order, the winner marked, the turn count, the elapsed time, and — at last — the full notes, which the page had simply never shown. The live tracker is untouched for games still in progress. We also began quietly noting when a game ends, so its playtime can be told; older games, whose endings we never timed, leave that line as a dash rather than guess.

resolved: Signing in is now case-insensitive for your email address, and editing your email on the account page stores it in a consistent lowercase form — so a stray capital letter can never lock you out of your own account.
archivist_note: A quiet safeguard against a trap we'd left half-open. The archive had always tidied your email to lowercase when you registered or asked for a password reset, but the account page's edit-email field didn't, and sign-in insisted on an exact match — so an account changed to "Jason@…" could leave you locked out the next time you typed "jason@…" out of habit. Now every door treats your email the same way: stored in one canonical form, and recognised whatever case you happen to type. The feature behind it — editing your email and display name — turned out to have been quietly working all along; this is the corner of it we hadn't swept.

added: Deck variant groups: link two or more builds of the same deck — say an Atraxa v1 and v2 — that share a single physical copy of many cards. Group them on the deck's edit panel, and when you import one build's list the archive treats a card already held by a sibling build as covered, instead of telling you to buy another copy.
archivist_note: A long-standing awkwardness, finally answered. The archive has always held to one quiet rule — a physical card lives in exactly one place — which serves it well until you're the sort of player who keeps two versions of the same deck and shuffles the shared cards between them depending on what you're in the mood to play. To the archive those looked like two separate wants for a card you only own once, and an import would dutifully insist you were short. Variant groups teach it the truth without bending the rule: the card still lives in one deck, but the group knows the builds are kin, so a card sitting in one is counted as covered for the other. We deliberately kept this to bookkeeping — no phantom shared shelf, no card cloned into two places at once — just the archive finally understanding that two builds can be one collection.

added: Add a single card to a location without the import funnel: open a location, click "+ Quick add," search a card, pick the printing, set finish/quantity/language/proxy/notes, and Save & Add More to keep going or Save & Close when you're done. The card and the location's totals update on the spot.
archivist_note: Until now, filing a single card you'd just picked up meant a trip through the whole import machinery — fine for a boxful, absurd for one. A location could hold cards, move them, and delete them, but it had no way to simply take a new one in. This release gives every location an open door: a quick-add panel that searches printings as you type, lets you set the handful of details we track, and offers a choice between settling in for a stack ("Save & Add More," which keeps your finish and quantity and location ready for the next) or filing just the one and closing up. We kept it to the fields the archive actually records — no pretend price-and-condition ledger — and the grid and its running totals refresh the moment a card lands.

refined: The card search inside a Showcase — and inside a shared, read-only view — now speaks the same language as the main Collection search bar. Type a query like t:creature, id:gw, c:r, is:foil, or cmc>=4, and combine them with OR, NOT, and parentheses, instead of just matching a card's name.
archivist_note: A small sharpening of yesterday's addition. When we made Showcases and Shares searchable, the box inside a showcase only matched on a card's name — serviceable, but a step behind the rich query language the Collection page has long understood. Now they're the same tool: ask a showcase for its red instants, its colourless artifacts, its four-drops, in exactly the syntax you'd use anywhere else in the archive. The list pages still search by plain name, as they should — there are no card types in a list of list names. And because the read-only shared view runs your query before it draws the curated cards, a search can only narrow what was already on display; it can never coax out a detail the owner chose to keep private.

added: Showcases and Shares are now searchable: filter your list of showcases or shares by name, and filter the cards inside any showcase — or a read-only shared view — by card name, all instantly as you type.

The Collection color filter now thinks like a Commander player. Pick the colors of a deck and you'll see every card that's legal in it — including colorless staples like Sol Ring, which the old filter wrongly hid.
archivist_note: Three small, deliberate improvements gathered into one quiet release. The headline is a correction of intuition: ask the Collection for your green cards and, until now, it took you literally — only cards whose identity was green, nothing broader, and pointedly not the colorless artifacts every green deck runs. But a Magic player who picks "green" almost always means "things I could put in a green deck," and that's the question the filter now answers. Alongside it, the Showcases and Shares pages — which can hold a great many cards once you've curated a while — gained search boxes that filter the moment you start typing. And behind the scenes, a wider safety net: a test that simply visits every page a signed-in collector can reach, so the kind of overlooked corner that slipped past us once before has a harder time doing so again.

added: You can now rename a player on a recorded game — fix a typo, or turn a placeholder like "Player 2" into a real name — right alongside linking the seat to an account.
resolved: The "Players & sharing" controls from the last release were there but unreachable — tucked beneath the full-screen game view with no way to scroll to them. They now open in a tidy panel from a "Players" button at the top of any game you recorded, on finished games as well as live ones.
archivist_note: A quick correction to the release that introduced shared games. The tools to attribute seats and share a game shipped, but they'd been placed in a spot the page's full-screen layout quietly swallowed — present in the markup, invisible in practice. So this is half repair, half completion: the controls now live behind a plain "Players" button in the game's top bar and open as a panel, and while we were in there we taught them one more trick the first cut lacked — editing the player's name itself, not just which account it points to. A name jotted down mid-game as "Player 2" can finally become the person who sat there.

added: Every player in a game can now see that game's record — not just whoever logged it. A game opens up to anyone linked to one of its seats, and, if you choose, to a whole playgroup; everyone else still can't see it.

You can go back and fix who played. On any game you recorded, link each seat to a real account — handy for games first jotted down with just names — and the moment you do, that player can view the record. You can also share a finished game with one of your playgroups in a single pick.
refined: The games list now marks the games you played in (but didn't record) so they're easy to tell apart from your own, and it keeps editing controls to the owner. A shared game you're viewing shows a quiet 'read-only' note up top.
archivist_note: A game was always recorded by one person, and so only that person could ever see it — even though three or four people sat at the table. This release lets the record belong, in a read-only way, to everyone who was there. The mechanism was already half-built: each seat could name the account that filled it, the app just never used that link to decide who may look. Now it does — owner, anyone seated, and, optionally, a whole playgroup. Editing stays with the one who recorded it; sharing is not the same as handing over the pen. And because plenty of older games were written down with nothing but names — a draft night, a kitchen-table pod — the owner can now go back and attach the real players after the fact, which is the moment those players gain the ability to see it. Nothing about the live game-tracker's saved state changed; this is purely about who is allowed through the door to read what's already written.

resolved: Signing in could fail with 'Invalid CSRF token' and leave you stuck on the sign-in page no matter how many times you tried. It now recovers on its own: if your browser reached the form without a valid session, the page quietly refreshes itself and your next sign-in goes through. Genuinely bad submissions are still refused, so the protection stays intact.
archivist_note: The sign-in form protects itself with a token: the page hands your browser a secret, and the form has to hand the same secret back. That stops a hostile site from submitting the form on your behalf. The check is sound — but it assumed the browser would always still be holding the secret by the time it submitted, and a browser arriving with a stale, expired, or wrong-host session held nothing to match. The form had no choice but to refuse, and refusing didn't repair the missing secret, so an honest person could be locked out for good. The application code along this path hadn't actually changed from the prior, working line; what changed around the release — a new public landing page and a second public address — was enough to leave some browsers turning up empty-handed. The fix teaches just the public, not-yet-signed-in forms to tell two cases apart: a mismatched secret (still refused, because that's the suspicious one) versus no secret at all (a fresh start — so the form re-issues one and invites you to try again). There is nothing to protect before you've signed in, so handing out a new secret costs nothing and unsticks the door. A new end-to-end test now drives the empty-session sign-in the whole way through, so this particular lock-out can't return unseen.

resolved: Viewing a Showcase that someone shared with your playgroup could fail with an 'Internal Server Error' instead of showing the cards. The shared view now loads correctly, total value and all. It was a one-line omission, and it's now covered by a new automated test that loads a shared view end-to-end so it can't slip back in.
archivist_note: A one-line omission with an outsized blast radius. The previous release taught the shared-Showcase view to show a total value, and added that value in two of the three places it was needed — the calculation, and the page that displays it — but not the third: the hand-off between them. So the page asked the server for a number it had never been handed, and the template, asked to format nothing, refused with an error. The fix is simply the missing line. The lesson is the test: our pre-release checks had opened the Showcase manager and the list of shares, but never a shared view itself, so the gap sat one click away from everything we looked at. The new test walks a shared view the whole way through — from the request to the finished page — and would have caught it cold. The same release also quietly tidies two workshop-only things no reader ever sees: a flaky internal test made dependable, and the development build's version label now showing its intended number rather than a commit hash.

added: You can now keep more than one Showcase. Build a trade binder, a brag list, a sell pile — as many curated lists as you like — and share any of them, read-only, with a playgroup. A new Showcases page lists them all with a card count and total value, and you pick which one when you add a card or set up a share.

Filling a Showcase no longer means adding cards one at a time. Open a Showcase and use 'Add my whole collection' or 'Add everything from this location' to bulk-fill it in one click. Cards already in the Showcase are skipped, and unconfirmed (pending) cards are left out.
Showcases and the read-only shared view now show a total value near the top, so you — and anyone you share with — can see at a glance what a curated list is worth.
refined: The Collection color filter behaves the way it should now. Clicking a mana pip highlights it instantly, picking several colors filters by all of them, and deselecting one actually clears it. Applying a filter change always shows fresh results — a browser-caching quirk could previously leave a stale view on screen even though the filter had changed.

Adding cards during an import is smoother: the 'Create & select' popup for a new deck or location now closes itself and immediately updates the reconciliation preview, and the duplicate-cards warning was reworded so it's clear that skipping cards you already own is the normal, expected choice — not something to worry about.
archivist_note: The Showcase began life deliberately constrained — one per person, the simplest thing that could work, with a note left in the code that a later release would lift the cap 'with no other change.' This is that release, and the note held: the one-per-user limit turned out to be a single database index, and removing it was a single line. Everything else this release is the ergonomics that grew around that one line — a page to hold many lists, a picker wherever you add or share a card, and a bulk fill so a whole binder, or your entire collection, becomes a Showcase in one motion instead of one card at a time. The rest is polish earned in use. A collector importing his entire collection found the color filter's pips didn't quite behave, the import popovers didn't close on their own, a warning read as an alarm when it meant reassurance — each fixed while he watched. The quietest fix is the most general: pages are now marked do-not-cache, because a filter you just changed should always show you the result, never yesterday's. Two things his session asked for that we deliberately did not build today — decks that share a single physical copy between two versions, and a Moxfield-style add-one-card-at-a-time panel — are written down for next time, not forgotten.

resolved: Hardened the collection database against a storage-layer corruption fault. Twice in two days the underlying database file came back damaged after an infrastructure restart interrupted a write mid-flight. The database now journals its writes in a crash-safe mode that is replayed cleanly the next time it opens, and it flushes and checkpoints itself on shutdown — so an unclean restart leaves a recoverable database instead of a corrupted one. Nothing changes in what you see or do; your data is simply harder to break.
archivist_note: Not a feature — a reinforcement. The application keeps everything in a single database file, and twice in one week that file returned damaged after the machinery beneath it was restarted at the wrong instant, a half-finished write still in the air. The repair is quieter than the incident: the database was switched to a write-ahead journaling mode, where each change is first appended to a side log and only later folded into the main file. If a restart interrupts that, the next startup simply replays the log and carries on — a recoverable state, never a malformed one. On a clean shutdown the service now also stops its background workers, folds the log back in, and closes the file deliberately, so the file at rest is whole. Alongside it the service began exposing operational metrics — invisible to readers, but the difference between watching the system and guessing at it. The durable answer to this whole class of fault is a different database engine, and that migration is now scheduled; this is the seatbelt until it lands.

resolved: Adding a card to a deck no longer 500s. Switching a card's printing no longer 500s. Both routes were broken on production for users who triggered a card the local cache hadn't seen before — the page just errored. The fix is a three-line strip at the two Card-construction sites; behavior on every other surface is unchanged.
archivist_note: A latent bug, lurking in the codebase since the eighth week of the produced_tokens column work. Three releases on that column had shipped without anyone noticing — the daemon wrote it correctly, the deck-detail Tokens panel read it correctly, the goldfish quick-add panel read it correctly. All three exercised the column on cards that already lived in the local cache. The two routes that finally failed — add a card to a deck, switch a card's printing — were the only ones that fetched a fresh card from Scryfall and tried to construct a Card row out of it. The freshly-fetched payload had 22 fields; the Card database table has 21. The 22nd field, produced_tokens, is scryfall_cards-only by design; it never belonged on a Card row. Two short call-site changes restore both routes. The lesson recorded in our notes: when extending the seam between Scryfall's data and our local card table, test the cache-miss code path explicitly. Pre-seeded test rows mask the bug because they hit cache-hit paths that don't go through the Card constructor.

added: When you open a deck in the goldfish playtester, all the tokens that the cards in the deck produce are now instantly available as quick-add buttons — no need to manually track them first. Cast Chatterfang and click '+ Squirrel × 1'. Tap a Treasure-maker and click '+ Treasure × 1'. The buttons are there waiting for you, with the correct art and type lines, the moment the playtester loads.

If you had manually-tracked tokens for a deck before, they still work. The auto-detected list is the primary path; anything you've curated by hand fills in for tokens the auto-detect doesn't cover (non-standard tokens, special-case productions, the occasional card the daemon hasn't backfilled yet).
refined: On a token that's both produced by a card in your deck AND manually tracked, the auto-detected entry wins. This means the quantity-needed shown reflects the deck's actual production reach, not whatever quantity you may have typed in when curating the row by hand. Manual quantity tracking still works for tokens the auto-detect path doesn't cover.
resolved: Closes the loop on the token data platform work. v3.30.11 added a column to the local card cache to record which tokens each card produces. v3.30.19 made the deck-detail Tokens panel read that column locally instead of calling Scryfall. v3.30.21 now uses the same column to auto-populate the goldfish playtester's quick-add panel. Three releases, one column, end-to-end coverage: data plumbing, request-path retirement, user-visible feature.
archivist_note: When we shipped the produced_tokens column eight weeks ago, the plan was a two-release sequence: data, then consumer. The consumer flip waited patiently while seven other releases jumped ahead of it — and arrived as a quiet local-read replacement that produced the exact same Tokens panel on deck-detail, just faster. What the original plan didn't capture was that the column would unlock a SECOND consumer: the goldfish playtester quick-add panel, which had until now required users to manually curate which tokens to track. Today's release reads the same column for a different reader. Open a deck in goldfish and the quick-add buttons are just there — Squirrel, Treasure, Food, Soldier, whatever the deck actually makes. Manual curation still works for the edge cases the auto-detect doesn't cover. The fix touched only three files; the load-bearing discipline was getting the new function to return the exact same dictionary shape the existing inline reader was already assembling, so the template and downstream enrichment didn't have to change. The wider lesson: a daemon-fed local column is a reusable platform. v3.30.11 paid for it once; v3.30.19 and v3.30.21 are two consumers and there could be more. The next time we want to leverage scryfall data without a request-path call, we have a pattern.

added: The import preview now warns you when a deck name you've ticked to create is already held by another user on the server. A small note appears next to the row — 'Another user on this server already has a deck named X' — and the row's checkbox is disabled so the import can't try to create it. You'll know before you click commit which decks will be skipped and where their cards will end up (in Pending, where you can place them manually).

The result page after import now lists any deck names that were skipped due to legacy name conflicts. If a row's name is still held by another user even after the preview warning (for example, via a tampered form submission), the cards land in Pending and the result page shows you which decks were affected and explains why.
refined: For CSV imports that don't carry a Location Type column (the common third-party-CSV case), the 'deck' option is hidden from a row's type dropdown whenever the name conflicts with another user's deck. The dropdown shows binder/box/drawer/other instead, with a small note explaining why deck is missing. You can still pick a non-deck type for that location — or rename the row to designate it as a deck.
resolved: Closes a quiet UX gap reported on prod: importing a CSV with seven deck-create checkboxes ticked, only one deck (a pre-existing one, reused via the existing-deck check) actually appeared on the Decks page. The other six names happened to be held by other users on the server, and the v3.30.18 defensive fallback correctly handled each constraint hit by skipping the row — but the skip was invisible to the importing user. v3.30.20 surfaces these collisions at preview time so the user can act before clicking commit, and on the result page so they can see which rows landed in Pending instead of becoming decks.
archivist_note: The investigation was the interesting part. The bug report suspected two code defects — either the session rollback was too broad (corrupting the batch transaction) or the per-user existing-deck check was returning false positives. A 25-assertion HTTP repro exhaustively tested both candidates and refuted both. The rollback recovers cleanly; non-conflicting decks after the failure still create successfully; the per-user check is correctly user-scoped. What looked like a code bug was actually correct behavior with no surface — the legacy single-column UNIQUE constraint on deck names (a v3.0-era artifact predating the multi-user database) was silently filtering out every deck name held by anyone else on the server, and the importing user had no way to know which ones would be skipped. The fix doesn't change any behavior; it just makes the existing behavior visible. The constraint itself will be removed in the v4 database upgrade, at which point the v3.30.20 warnings, the v3.30.18 fallback, and the cross-user lookup field all become safe to delete together. Until then: surface the silent, so the user can act.

added: Opening a deck no longer calls Scryfall. Token data — the produced-tokens list, the art, the type line, the set and collector number — now comes from the local card cache. Deck-detail pages render the Tokens panel without any network round-trip; warm or cold cache, the answer is the same.
refined: The change is invisible to you. The token panel renders the same tokens, sorted the same way, with the same art and details as before. The only difference is where the data came from: yesterday Scryfall over HTTPS; today the local database.
resolved: The deck-detail token panel was the last surviving request-path Scryfall call in the app. v3.27.9 retired the combos-panel call; v3.27.13 retired the set-completion call; v3.30.19 retires this third one. No Cartarch page now reaches out to Scryfall while you wait. The card data still comes from Scryfall — through the nightly background sync that has populated the bulk cache since v3.25.0 — but the request path itself is fully local.
archivist_note: Eight weeks ago we added a new column to the card cache to make this possible — `produced_tokens`, capturing which tokens each card produces. The column waited patiently for its consumer to ship, while seven other releases jumped ahead of it: counter-pill renders, click-to-adjust ergonomics, drag-attach, two data-loss fixes for the importer, an export schema expansion, and a corrective patch on top of that. Each preempt was a real bug that needed shipping first; the consumer flip wasn't slipping, it was waiting its turn. With the prod backfill at 114,242 of 114,242 rows (every card populated, none remaining), the flip dropped in cleanly — same Tokens panel, same dedup, same art, just sourced locally now. The v3.27.9 release-history note that explicitly tolerated this call — 'kept; the result is cached so worst-case latency is bounded' — is now resolved. The panels-cache stays for warm-load speed; it is no longer load-bearing for network avoidance. Next: open. The major v3.30.x series — the goldfish playtester feedback loop and the round-trip importer/exporter — are both complete. The owner picks the next slot.

added: Third-party CSVs (Moxfield, Deckbox, hand-crafted lists) can now designate a location as a deck at import time. v3.30.17 had been too aggressive about removing the deck option from the auto-create dropdown for CSVs without a Location Type column — leaving these third-party imports with no in-import path to create a deck. v3.30.18 restores the deck option for rows whose CSV didn't specify a type. Selecting it converts the row in-place to a per-row 'Create deck X' checkbox, same shape as the Cartarch-export path.
refined: CSV rows whose Location Type was explicitly set to something non-deck (binder / box / drawer / other) keep the v3.30.17 behaviour: the dropdown shows the CSV-supplied type pre-selected and does NOT offer deck. Re-classifying an explicit CSV choice from non-deck to deck wasn't a real workflow — better to keep the CSV's intent visible.
resolved: Closes a real-prod 500 on /import/commit when a CSV happened to use a deck name that another user already owned. The root cause was a pre-v3.1.0 legacy UNIQUE constraint on deck names alone (predating multi-tenancy) that SQLite cannot drop in-place — a full table rebuild deferred to v4. v3.30.18 wraps the deck creation in a try/except that catches this constraint, rolls the session back cleanly, and skips the resolution (cards fall through to Pending). The rest of the import batch continues working. Self-heals when v4's table rebuild drops the legacy constraint.
archivist_note: The v3.30.17 release went out earlier today with two issues the owner caught in verification: a dropdown that quietly broke the third-party-CSV path (most real-world imports lack a Location Type column), and a legacy UNIQUE constraint from before the project was multi-user that surfaced as a 500 on real production data. The first was a scoping miss — easy to fix once flagged. The second was a deeper find: the SQLite error message format gave it away (single-column constraints emit a different shape than compound ones), and `git log -S` on the constraint name traced it back to a v3.1.0 migration that added a multi-user constraint but never dropped its single-user predecessor. SQLite can't drop an inline-UNIQUE auto-index without rebuilding the whole table; the proper cleanup waits for the v4 PostgreSQL migration. For now, a three-line try/except handles the constraint defensively — and the rest of the import batch continues working when one name conflicts. The v3.30.17 tag still exists locally as a dev-process artifact; v3.30.18 is what deploys. Next stop, finally, is the long-deferred fetch_deck_tokens consumer flip (now v3.30.19 — re-numbered seven times).

added: Importing a collection backup now correctly creates real Deck records for deck-type locations. A row whose Location Type is 'deck' (the natural shape of a /collection/export of a user with decks) auto-creates a paired Deck record alongside the StorageLocation, so the deck appears on the Decks page after a round-trip restore, has goldfish + analytics, and is reachable from every deck-facing surface.

The preview step now shows deck creations distinctly. Decks-to-be-created render in their own panel with per-row 'Create deck X' opt-in checkboxes — pick exactly which decks you want to materialize from the import. Unchecked decks send their cards to Pending instead, which you can place manually afterward.

Cards heading into an existing deck during import surface a small informational note. 'Cards with Location=X will be placed into your existing deck X' — no confirm gate, just a heads-up that those rows are landing in a deck you already have.
refined: Auto-created decks ship with no format set. The deck appears on the Decks page; you can edit the format afterward via the existing Decks page Edit popout. Carrying deck format through the export schema is a separate follow-up.

The non-deck Type dropdown in the auto-create confirm panel no longer offers 'deck' as an option. Deck creation now happens exclusively through the dedicated deck-create panel (with its per-row opt-in checkboxes). Any CSV row asking for 'deck' creates a real Deck record, or, if you uncheck, sends its cards to Pending.

Deck creation now never goes through the generic create_location code path. A small architectural guard: if any code ever attempts to create a StorageLocation with type='deck' through create_location, the call fails loudly instead of producing a half-finished deck.
resolved: Closes a latent data-loss class introduced by v3.30.15/.16. The auto-create path could previously create a StorageLocation marked as a deck without ever creating the paired Deck record — an orphan invisible to the Decks page, goldfish, and analytics, even though the cards sat in the location. A diagnostic query against prod confirmed nobody had triggered the bug yet (the confirm gate held). This release closes the hole before any user is affected.
archivist_note: The v3.30.15 importer fix introduced the bypass; the v3.30.16 export schema expansion made it easier to hit by defaulting the Location Type dropdown to 'deck' whenever the CSV said so. Two days, two improvements, one quiet hole between them. The recon ran a diagnostic query against the live database and found zero orphans — caught before harm, exactly the use case the diagnostic was for. v3.30.17 routes deck-type auto-create through the canonical Deck-creation path so the paired Deck row lands atomically alongside the StorageLocation, restoring the v3.3 architectural invariant that has held since the codebase first treated decks as storage locations. Next: the long-deferred fetch_deck_tokens consumer flip (still v3.30.18; the v3.30.11 daemon backfill needs to finish first).

added: Collection export now carries five additional columns alongside the existing six: Location Type, Language, Role, Tags, and Is Proxy. Existing six columns stay in their existing positions and contents — downstream parsers that read the previous shape keep working byte-identically; new columns are appended at the end.

Per-row Location Type is honored on re-import. When the importer auto-creates a missing location, it now uses the CSV-supplied type (binder, box, drawer, deck, other) instead of defaulting everything to 'other'. The preview's auto-create confirm panel surfaces a dropdown per name — defaulted from the CSV value, editable inline if you want to override.

Per-row Language, Role, Tags, and Is Proxy round-trip exactly. A Japanese-language proxy commander in a deck exports as one row and re-imports as one row with the same finish, language, role, tags, and proxy flag. Tags are preserved verbatim — the importer does not interpret them.
refined: Round-trip into a fresh account becomes meaningful. With v3.30.15 honoring per-row Location and v3.30.16 carrying the metadata, you can export your collection and re-import it into a new user account and get back the same locations (with the right types), same commanders, same proxies, same languages, same tags.

Old six-column CSVs continue to import cleanly under v3.30.16. Missing new columns default to their model defaults (Language → 'en', Is Proxy → False, Role / Tags → NULL). Nothing about the v3.30.15 importer behaviour regressed.

Existing location's type wins silently when a CSV Location Type disagrees with an existing StorageLocation. If your CSV claims 'Mythics Binder' is type='box' but you already have a 'Mythics Binder' marked type='binder', the import puts the row into your existing binder and the binder's type stays as binder. No warning; your data is canonical (the CSV value is only consulted when auto-creating a location that doesn't yet exist).

Invalid Role / Is Proxy values surface in the existing Invalid Rows panel with explicit reasons (e.g. 'Invalid Role value: "frodo" (allowed: empty or commander)'). No silent coercion. You either fix the CSV and re-import, or accept that those rows are skipped while the valid rows commit.

Invalid Location Type values trigger an inline dropdown in the auto-create confirm panel. The dropdown defaults to 'other' and shows a small warning noting that the CSV value was not recognized. Pick a valid type from the list — or just accept 'other' — and continue.
resolved: Closes the round-trip fidelity gap. v3.30.15 + v3.30.16 together resolve the long-standing /collection/export → /import data-loss report: locations preserved (v3.30.15), languages preserved (v3.30.16), commander tags preserved (v3.30.16), proxies preserved (v3.30.16), tags preserved (v3.30.16), and auto-created location types preserved (v3.30.16). Exporting your collection now genuinely produces a backup you can restore from.
archivist_note: The pair shipped a day apart by design — v3.30.15 made the importer ready to honor per-row Location data, v3.30.16 made the exporter emit it. The order matters: making backup easier without fixing the import side would have widened the original trap, not closed it. Now both halves are in place. A user who carefully tagged their commanders, marked their proxies, and wrote tags on their cards can export and re-import and get back the same picture. One known limitation carries forward from v3.30.15: re-importing the SAME CSV twice still adds quantities to your existing rows rather than replacing them (a 2-qty Sol Ring import on top of an existing 2-qty becomes 4, not 2). The merge key is load-bearing logic for every import path, not just round-trip; refining it needs its own focused design conversation, recorded as the Round-trip merge semantics follow-up. The next stop, finally, is the long-deferred fetch_deck_tokens consumer flip — gated on the v3.30.11 daemon backfill having populated the bulk cache.

added: Per-row location resolution in the CSV importer. When a CSV carries a Location column (as your collection export does), each row's destination is now resolved against your storage locations and the cards land directly placed in their original homes — no more pending queue, no more single-destination collapse on re-import. The preview step shows you the resolutions before you commit so nothing surprises you.

Ambiguous-name picker in the preview step. If a Location name in your CSV matches two or more of your storage locations (you have more than one 'Bulk'), the preview gives you a dropdown to pick which one each name resolves to.

Missing-name auto-create with explicit confirm. If a Location name doesn't match anything you have, the preview offers to create it for you (as 'other' type — edit later on the Locations page). You have to check the confirm box; cancelling rejects the whole batch without writing anything.

Duplicate-merge warning in the preview step. When importing rows match cards you already have placed in the resolved location, the preview tells you up front — those quantities will be added to your existing copies.
refined: Blank-Location CSVs (new-acquisition scans, no round-trip context) behave exactly as before — rows land pending and the batch-wide destination dropdown applies. No regression to the new-acquisitions workflow.

Drawer-sorter users with mixed-Location CSVs get the right of both worlds: rows with resolved Locations land directly in those locations (bypassing auto-sort); rows without per-row Location fall through to the existing auto-sort or pending behavior.

Notes field is no longer hijacked by the importer. Pre-v3.30.15 importing wrote the Location string into the notes field; v3.30.15 stops doing that. Your existing rows with location-strings in notes from past imports are NOT touched — that data is yours.
